name: Jormungandr Extended
description: Jormungandr, also known as the Midgard Serpent, is a colossal sea serpent, one of the three monstrous offspring of Loki and the giantess Angrboða, along with the wolf Fenrir and the goddess Hel. Cast into the ocean surrounding Midgard by Odin, Jormungandr grew to such immense size that it encircled the entire Earth, grasping its own tail in its mouth. This self-embracing posture maintains the balance of the world, and any disturbance to it heralds the arrival of Ragnarök, the end of days. Jormungandr's venom is incredibly potent, capable of poisoning entire armies and polluting the seas. Its movements cause earthquakes and tidal waves, showcasing its immense power. During Ragnarök, Jormungandr will rise from the ocean, poisoning the sky and sea with its venom, and engage in a deadly battle with Thor, god of thunder. Though Thor will ultimately slay the serpent, he will succumb to its venom shortly after, marking a tragic end for both combatants. The serpent represents chaos, destruction, and the cyclical nature of the cosmos, a force that must be confronted but cannot be permanently defeated. The extended aspect refers to interpretations that depict Jormungandr as a symbol of the interconnectedness of all things and the destructive potential of unchecked growth and ambition. Its existence serves as a constant reminder of the fragility of order and the inevitability of chaos in the Norse cosmos. Some interpretations also highlight the ecological impact of such a creature, considering the disruption it would cause to the marine environment and the broader ecosystem. Jormungandr embodies the untamed power of nature and the constant threat it poses to the established order.
subtitle: World Serpent, coiled around Midgard
appearance: Jormungandr is depicted as a serpent of immense size, so large that it encircles the entire world. Its scales are typically described as being dark green or black, blending in with the depths of the ocean. Some accounts portray it with bioluminescent markings, illuminating the dark waters. Its eyes are said to be cold and calculating, reflecting the ancient wisdom and destructive potential it possesses. Its massive jaws are capable of swallowing entire ships whole, and its fangs drip with potent venom. The serpent's movements are described as fluid and powerful, creating massive waves and whirlpools as it navigates the ocean depths.
habitat: Jormungandr resides in the vast ocean that surrounds Midgard, the realm of humans. It dwells in the deepest trenches and unexplored regions of the sea, hidden from the eyes of mortals. The serpent's presence permeates the entire ocean, influencing its currents and shaping its ecology.
behavior: Jormungandr is generally dormant, remaining coiled in the depths of the ocean, patiently waiting for the arrival of Ragnarök. However, it can be roused by disturbances in the world, such as earthquakes or volcanic eruptions. When awakened, it becomes a destructive force, unleashing its venom and causing chaos and destruction. It is driven by instinct and a primal urge to fulfill its role in the apocalyptic events of Ragnarök.

keyMyths.title: The Casting of Jormungandr
keyMyths.description: Odin, recognizing the threat posed by Loki's monstrous offspring, cast Jormungandr into the ocean surrounding Midgard. This act was intended to contain the serpent's power, but it ultimately led to the fulfillment of its destiny in Ragnarök.

keyMyths.title: Thor's Fishing Trip
keyMyths.description: Thor, disguised as a young man, went fishing with the giant Hymir. He used the head of an ox as bait and managed to hook Jormungandr, nearly pulling the serpent from the depths before Hymir, fearing the consequences, cut the line.

keyMyths.title: Ragnarök
keyMyths.description: During Ragnarök, Jormungandr rises from the ocean, poisoning the world with its venom and battling Thor. Both are ultimately slain, signifying the end of the old world and the beginning of a new one.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A major source of Norse mythology, detailing the creation of the world, the gods, and the events of Ragnarök.
